results.possibleIdentity: Ronald Chase (December 29, 1934 – December 20, 2025) was an American multidisciplinary artist, filmmaker, and opera designer based in San Francisco. He is celebrated as a pioneer of "film-opera," a technique he developed in the late 1960s that integrated film projections and slides onto scrims during live operatic performances. His breakthrough in this medium occurred in 1968 with a production of Benjamin Britten's 'The Turn of the Screw' for the Washington Opera. Chase was active in the experimental art scene of the late 1960s and early 1970s, collaborating with notable figures like composer Pauline Oliveros on the dance film 'The Covenant' (1965). His association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) milieu is evidenced by his presence in experimental archives and his work's inclusion in contexts related to the E.A.T. publication 'Techne' (1970). Beyond opera, Chase was an accomplished painter and photographer whose work is held in the collections of the Metropolitan Museum of Art and SFMOMA. In 1972, he filmed 'Parade,' a documentary of San Francisco's first Gay Pride Parade. He was also a dedicated educator, founding the San Francisco Art & Film Program for Teenagers in 1993.

results.possibleIdentity: Alan Kalker is a multi-disciplinary artist and technologist active from the late 1960s to the present. He was involved in the countercultural art and technology scene of the San Francisco Bay Area during the 1960s and 1970s. In January 1971, he participated in the "Reject's art show" at 165 Grove Street in San Francisco alongside artists such as Calvin Tondre and Marjorie Mount. His work in this era often blended art with technical processes; for instance, he registered a copyright for a "Geometrical Model" as a work of art in 1973. Kalker was a contributor and associate of Stewart Brand’s Whole Earth publications, appearing in "The Last Supplement to The Whole Earth Catalog" (1971) and later issues of "CoEvolution Quarterly" and "Whole Earth Review." By the 1980s, he was associated with "Microsculptor" in Berkeley, California, where he worked with early microcomputer graphics and hardware (such as 68000 systems). His profile is consistent with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization dedicated to collaborations between artists and engineers. In his later career, Kalker relocated to Madison, Wisconsin, where he became a professional furniture maker under the name Alan's Fine Woodworking, specializing in functional, heirloom-quality wood art. He is an alumnus of the Center for Furniture Craftsmanship (2013) and is married to chemical engineer and author Abigail F. Cantor.

results.possibleIdentity: Kent Holloway (1935–2004) was an American artist active in the 1960s and 1970s, known for both traditional oil painting and experimental works that bridged art and technology. He was associated with the San Francisco and Portland bohemian art scenes, appearing in the records of North Beach beat poet ruth weiss and the journals of Philip Whalen, who described his style in the early 1960s as symbolist painting characterized by a "thick overlay" and a "resurrection of ancestral totems." Holloway was notably involved with a group interested in Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), collaborating with artist Bob Miller at the Exploratorium in San Francisco. Together, they developed early exhibits such as light-based "sun-paintings" that used prisms and tracked the sun to create strokes of color in the air. He is also credited with creating a "sun-tracking painting machine" and was a contemporary and associate of claymation pioneer Bob Gardiner. His traditional works include portraits, landscapes, and nudes, often signed "K. Holloway" or "Kent C. Holloway."

results.possibleIdentity: Jack Ward (also known as John Ward) is an American artist and sculptor who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s, with a career that extended into later decades. He is specifically recognized for his modernist, geometric sculptures and his participation in significant exhibitions that explored the intersection of color and form. In June and July 1971, he was part of the 'Color' exhibition at the Palo Alto Cultural Center in California, alongside other notable artists such as Sam Richardson and Gurdon Woods. He also participated in the '1st Annual Sculpture Invitational' in Palo Alto in early 1973. In 1974, Ward was commissioned by the City of Detroit to create a monumental public sculpture for Gordon Park, located at the corner of 12th and Clairmount Streets (the site where the 1967 Detroit rebellion began). His sculpture, originally titled 'Untitled' and later renamed 'Detroit 1967' in the 1990s, is a polyhedron ironwork structure designed with a 'universal' abstract geometric form. His work in the Palo Alto art scene during the early 1970s placed him in a cultural hub known for collaborations between artists and technology researchers, a milieu closely aligned with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

Jack Ward is an electronic engineer and technical historian who has spent much of his career documenting and preserving the history of early electronics, specifically transistors. He is the principal of the 'Transistor Museum,' an online resource and physical collection dedicated to the historical development of semiconductors. Active in the electronics industry from the mid-20th century, Ward has collaborated extensively with the Computer History Museum (CHM) in Mountain View, California, donating numerous historic artifacts and contributing to oral history projects. His work involves interviewing pioneering engineers from organizations like Bell Labs and IBM, which were central to the collaborations fostered by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in the 1960s. While primarily known as an engineer and historian, his work in archiving the aesthetic and technical achievements of early computer technology represents a critical bridge between the arts, history, and science, matching the collaborative spirit of the E.A.T. organization.

results.possibleIdentity: Lotte Cherin is a Los Angeles-based artist, arts administrator, and technologist who has been active in the Southern California arts community since at least the late 1960s. She is perhaps best known in the historical context for her association with the electronic music pioneer Ruth White (1925–2013). Cherin currently holds the copyright and manages the legacy of White's seminal electronic works, including the 1969 albums "Flowers of Evil" and "7 Trumps from the Tarot Cards and Pinions," which were reissued in 2019 under her name. Her professional background bridges art and technology; she served as a Senior Client Technologies Analyst and User Consultant at Pepperdine University's Graduate School of Education and Psychology for many years. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, this intersection of disciplines aligned with the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Cherin was also an active participant in the Los Angeles feminist art movement, with artist records dated 1980–1985 held in the Woman's Building archives at the Getty Research Institute. She is a metalsmith and jeweler whose work was featured in the 1995 exhibition "ALCHEMY: Metals Made Precious" at Cabrillo College. More recently, she has served as the Chair of the Malibu Arts Commission, where she has advocated for public art and the establishment of a Malibu Arts Center.
